1: Isaiah 42:1 opens the First Servant Song (Isaiah 42:1-4), one of four Servant Songs in Second Isaiah (Isaiah 42:1-4; Isaiah 49:1-6; Isaiah 50:4-11; Isaiah 52:13-53:12): 'hen avdi etmokh-bo, behold my servant, I uphold him.' Three characteristics of the Servant: (1) 'my servant, whom I uphold' (avdi ethmokh-bo = my servant, I hold/support him) — divine backing; (2) 'my chosen one, in whom I delight' (behiiri ratsethah nafshi = my chosen one, in whom my soul delights) — the same 'soul delights' as Genesis 34:8 (Shechem's delight in Dinah), applied to YHWH's delight in the Servant; (3) 'I have placed my Spirit on him' (natati ruchi alaiv = I have put my Spirit on him) — equipping for ministry. The mission: 'mishpat laggoyim yotsi = justice to the nations he will bring forth.' The mishpat (justice/teaching/ordinance = the proper ordering of society according to YHWH's standard) brought to the nations (goyim = the gentile peoples). Matthew 12:18-21 quotes vv1-4 for Jesus. The Servant is simultaneously Israel (the corporate servant of Isaiah 41:8-9) and the individual who fulfills what Israel failed to do.
2: 'He will not shout or raise his voice, or make himself heard in the streets' — the quietness of the Servant's method: 'lo yitssaq velo yissa velo yashmia bachuuts qolo, he will not cry out and not lift and not make heard in the street his voice.' The three negatives of public clamor (crying out, lifting the voice, making heard in the street) = the full repertoire of public proclamation typical of (1) ANE royal heralds announcing conquests, (2) military commanders issuing battle orders, (3) marketplace disputants, (4) prophets in the temple gates (Jeremiah 7:2: 'stand in the gate of the LORD's house and proclaim there this word'). The Servant works without public clamor — the contrast with both the noise of Babylonian divine processions (where Bel and Nebo were carried through the streets with great fanfare, Isaiah 46:1-2) and even with the style of earlier prophets. Matthew 12:19 quotes this directly for Jesus's characteristic avoidance of public spectacle (Matthew 12:15-16: 'warned them not to say who he was').
3: 'A bruised reed he will not break, and a smoldering wick he will not snuff out; he will bring forth justice faithfully' — the Servant's treatment of the vulnerable: 'qaneh ratsuts lo yishbor ufishtat kehavah lo yekhabennah, a bruised reed he will not break, and a smoldering wick he will not extinguish.' Two images of extreme fragility: (1) qaneh ratsuts (bruised/cracked reed = a hollow river reed used as a musical instrument or writing tool that has been crushed or cracked — normally discarded as useless); (2) pishtat kehah (a dimly smoldering wick = the flax wick of an oil lamp that is barely burning, about to go out). The Servant's ministry preserves what human systems discard as non-functional. 'He will bring forth justice faithfully' (le'emet yotsi mishpat = in truth/faithfulness he will bring out justice) — the persistence of the justice-mission despite the gentleness of the method. Matthew 12:20 applies this to Jesus's healing ministry: the bruised reed = the chronically ill, socially marginal, spiritually depleted people he healed rather than dismissed.
4: 'He will not grow faint or be discouraged until he has established justice in the earth; and the coastlands will wait for his law' — the Servant's inexhaustibility and universal mission: 'lo yikheh velo yaruts, he will not grow faint and will not be crushed.' The precise inversion of the bruised reed and smoldering wick: the Servant himself will not be 'faint' (yikheh = the dim-wick word used in v3: kehah) nor 'crushed/broken' (yaruts = the same root as ratsuts of the bruised reed). He does not break the bruised reed because he himself does not break. He does not snuff out the smoldering wick because he himself is not snuffed out. 'Until he has established justice in the earth' (ad-yashim ba'arets mishpat = until he sets justice in the earth) — the temporal scope: the Servant persists until the task is complete. 'The coastlands will wait for his law' (vetor'atoh iyyim yeyahelu = and for his instruction the coastlands wait) — the universal expectation: the most distant peoples (coastlands = Isaiah 40:15; Isaiah 41:1, 5) are waiting for the Servant's teaching.
5: 'This is what God the LORD says — he who created the heavens and stretched them out, who spread out the earth and everything it produces, who gives breath to the people on it and spirit to those who walk on it' — the Creator's formal speech introducing the Servant commission: 'ko-amar ha'El YHWH bore hashamayim venotehem, thus says the God YHWH, creator of the heavens and the one who stretched them out.' The fourfold Creator-resume before commissioning the Servant: (1) 'bore hashamayim = creator of the heavens'; (2) 'venotehem = who stretched them out' (the spatial expansion of creation); (3) 'roqa ha'arets vetsetsaeyha = who spread out the earth and its offspring/produce'; (4) 'noten neshama la'am aleyhah veruach laholkhim bah = giving breath to the people on it and spirit to those walking on it.' The neshama (breath = the divine breath of Genesis 2:7: 'breathed into his nostrils the breath of life') given to all people — universal bestowal of life. The Servant is commissioned by the God who creates and sustains all life — his mission is therefore cosmic in scope.
6: 'I, the LORD, have called you in righteousness; I will take hold of your hand and keep you; I will make you a covenant for the people, a light for the nations' — the Servant's commission: 'ani YHWH qeratikha vetsedek veachazzeq beyadekha, I, YHWH, have called you in righteousness and will grip your hand.' Three commissioning acts: (1) 'called you in righteousness' (qeratikha vetsedek = called you for the purpose of righteousness/covenant-justice); (2) 'take hold of your hand' (ve'etchazzeq beyadekha = I will hold firmly your hand, cf. Isaiah 41:13: 'I hold your right hand') — divine physical support; (3) 'keep you' (ve'etsorkha = I will guard/preserve you). Two mission-roles: 'berit am = covenant for/of the people' (the Servant embodies or mediates the covenant between YHWH and Israel; the ambiguity is productive: he is both the content of the covenant and its mediator) and 'or goyim = light of the nations.' Luke 2:32 (Simeon): 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and the glory of your people Israel'; Acts 13:47 (Paul and Barnabas): 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles.'
7: 'To open eyes that are blind, to bring prisoners out of the dungeon, to release from prison those who sit in darkness' — the practical content of the Servant's mission: 'lifqoach einayim ivrot, to open blind eyes.' Three liberating acts: (1) 'open eyes that are blind' (lefqoach einayim ivrot = to open blind eyes) — literal blindness and the spiritual blindness of Isaiah 42:18-20; (2) 'bring prisoners out of the dungeon' (lehotsi mimmasgger asir = to bring out from the prison/dungeon the prisoner) — literal captives in Babylonian prisons and the exilic community as imprisoned; (3) 'release from prison those who sit in darkness' (mibeit kele yoshevei choshekh = from the house of imprisonment those sitting in darkness). The three-fold liberation = the comprehensive redemption from every form of captivity. Luke 4:18 (Jesus in Nazareth): 'he has sent me to proclaim freedom for the prisoners and recovery of sight for the blind' — quoting Isaiah 61:1-2 but echoing this passage. John 9:39: 'For judgment I have come into this world, so that the blind will see and those who see will become blind.'
8: 'I am the LORD; that is my name; I will not give my glory to another nor my praise to carved idols' — the divine identity assertion and the grounds for the Servant's mission: 'ani YHWH hu shemi, I, YHWH, that is my name.' The hu shemi (that is my name) = the definitive identification, not merely a description but a declaration of essential identity. 'I will not give my glory to another' (khevodi le'acher lo etten, my glory to another I will not give) — the divine jealousy that grounds the Servant's exclusive commission: the Servant brings the nations to the knowledge of YHWH's glory, not to any other deity's glory. 'Nor my praise to carved idols' (utehillati lafssilim = and my praise to carved idols) — the worship that the nations currently give to idols must be redirected to YHWH. The Servant's mission is fundamentally doxological: oriented toward the universal praise of the one true God.
9: 'See — the former things have come to pass, and new things I now declare; before they spring up I announce them to you' — the hermeneutic principle of Second Isaiah: 'harishonut hineh bau, the former things, behold they have come to pass.' The rishonut (former things = the earlier events of salvation history: Exodus, wilderness, conquest, Davidic kingdom, Assyrian crisis) have 'come to pass' (bau = they have come/happened = they are verified history). 'And new things I now declare' (vachadashot ani maggid = and new things I am announcing) = the hadashot (new things = the new Exodus from Babylon, the Servant's mission, the universal salvation) now being announced. 'Before they spring up I announce them to you' (biterem titsmachnah ashmi'a etkhem = before they sprout I let you hear them) — the prophetic word as advance announcement: YHWH speaks before the event springs up from the ground like new vegetation. The prediction-announcement pattern is YHWH's trademark (Isaiah 41:22-23: the challenge to the idols to predict; Isaiah 41:26: 'who declared this from the beginning?').
10: 'Sing to the LORD a new song, his praise from the ends of the earth — you who sail the sea and all its creatures, you coastlands and their inhabitants' — the new-song call: 'shiru laYHWH shir chadash, sing to YHWH a new song.' The shir chadash (new song = the song that matches a new act of salvation; Psalms 33:3; Isaiah 40:3; Isaiah 96:1; Isaiah 98:1; Isaiah 144:9; Isaiah 149:1; Revelation 5:9; Isaiah 14:3) is demanded because YHWH is doing a new thing (Isaiah 42:9; Isaiah 43:19). 'His praise from the ends of the earth' (tehillato miqtseh ha'arets = his praise from the end of the earth) — the geographic scope: the new song arises universally, not just from Jerusalem's temple. The choir: 'you who sail the sea' (yoredei hayam = those who go down to the sea = sailors/maritime traders; Psalms 107:23), 'all its creatures' (umelo'o = and all its fullness = the marine life; Psalms 96:11: 'let the sea resound and all that is in it'), 'you coastlands and their inhabitants' (iyyim veyosheveiyhem = the distant islands/coastlands and their inhabitants). The maritime trade-world as the first universal choir.
11: 'Let the wilderness and its towns raise their voice, the villages where Kedar dwells; let the inhabitants of Sela sing for joy; let them shout from the tops of the mountains' — the desert-world's choir: 'yiseu midbar ve'araiv chatserim, let the wilderness and its cities lift up their voices.' The contrast with v10's maritime world: now the desert peoples join. 'The villages where Kedar dwells' (chatserim teshev Qedar = the unwalled settlements where Kedar dwells) — Kedar (from Genesis 25:13, a son of Ishmael = the Arab tribal confederation of the north Arabian desert, known for their black tents, Psalms 120:5: 'I dwell in the tents of Kedar'; Song of Solomon 1:5: 'I am dark but lovely... like the tents of Kedar'). 'The inhabitants of Sela' (yoshevei sela = the inhabitants of the Rock = possibly Petra, the Nabataean capital in Edom, or a reference to rocky highlands generally). The desert peoples — nomadic Arabs and rock-dwellers — join the maritime peoples in the universal new song.
12: 'Let them ascribe glory to the LORD and proclaim his praise in the coastlands' — the doxological goal of the universal choir: 'yassimu laYHWH kavod vetehillato ba'iyyim yaggidu, let them ascribe to YHWH glory and his praise in the coastlands let them tell.' The kavod (glory = the weighty presence and honor due to YHWH; Isaiah 40:5: 'the glory of the LORD will be revealed and all flesh will see it together') ascribed universally. The 'coastlands' (iyyim) appear for the fourth time in Isaiah 42 (vv4, 10, 12) = the representative of the most distant, most 'other' people who are nevertheless included in the praise. The universal praise is not accidental but the designed outcome of the Servant's mission (Isaiah 42:6-7) and the new things YHWH is doing (Isaiah 42:9). The doxological purpose-statement of creation (Isaiah 43:7: 'I created for my glory') realized in the universal choir.
13: 'The LORD marches out like a warrior, like a man of war he arouses his zeal; he cries out, he shouts aloud, he shows himself mighty against his enemies' — the divine warrior's battle-march: 'YHWH keggibbor yetse, YHWH goes out like a warrior.' The gibbor (mighty warrior/champion = the strongest and most elite fighting man; Genesis 6:4: the 'Nephilim were the heroes/gibbor of old'; 2 Samuel 23:8-39: David's thirty mighty warriors). 'Like a man of war he arouses his zeal' (keish milchamot ya'ir qin'ah = like a man of wars he arouses/stirs up his jealousy). The qin'ah (zeal/jealousy = the passionate, exclusive commitment of a covenant partner; Isaiah 37:32: 'the zeal of YHWH of hosts will accomplish this'). 'He cries out, he shouts aloud' (yariya af-yatsriyach = he cries out, he also shouts/yells) — the battle cry of the divine warrior. The contrast with the quiet Servant (Isaiah 42:2: 'he will not shout or raise his voice') is held in tension: the Servant works quietly; YHWH the warrior acts loudly. Both are aspects of one redemptive plan.
14: 'For a long time I have kept silent; I have been still and held myself back; now I will cry out like a woman in labor; I will gasp and pant' — the divine patience breaking into action: 'me'olam hecherasheiti achrish et'apaq, from long ago I have kept silent, I have been still and held back.' The me'olam (from long ago/eternity) of YHWH's restraint = the entire period of the Babylonian exile (and before it, the Assyrian period) during which YHWH appeared silent. 'Now I will cry out like a woman in labor' (keyyoleydah eph'ah, like a woman giving birth I will groan/gasp) — the childbirth metaphor for YHWH's action: not the composed sovereign but the groaning woman in the final stages of labor. The imagery is stunning in its vulnerability: YHWH's powerful act of salvation is compared to the involuntary, pain-driven gasp of a woman delivering a child. 'I will gasp and pant' (esh'af ve'eshom = I will inhale and exhale rapidly) — the panting of extreme exertion. The new creation that YHWH is about to accomplish is as costly and involuntary (from the perspective of the divine love) as childbirth.
15: 'I will lay waste mountains and hills and dry up all their vegetation; I will turn rivers into dry land and drain the pools' — the new-Exodus geography inverted: 'achorev harim ugivot vekhol-esevam avish, I will devastate mountains and hills and dry up all their vegetation.' The reversal of Isaiah 40's creation-imagery (Isaiah 40:4: 'every mountain and hill leveled') continues: now YHWH also dries the rivers and pools (v15) while elsewhere creating new rivers (Isaiah 41:18). The apparently contradictory images (drying rivers in 42:15 vs. creating rivers in Isaiah 41:18; Isaiah 43:19-20) reflect different landscapes: the obstacle-landscape of the enemies is dried and flattened; the route of the returning exiles is watered and smoothed. 'I will turn rivers into dry land and drain the pools' (asim neharot le'iyyim ve'agammim ovish = I will make rivers into dry land and pools I will dry up) — the drying of Babylon's waterways (the Euphrates and the irrigation canals) is exactly what Cyrus did when he diverted the Euphrates to allow his troops to enter Babylon (Herodotus 1.189-191).
16: 'I will lead the blind along a road they have not known; I will guide them on paths they have never walked. I will turn darkness into light before them, and rough terrain into level ground. These are the things I will do, and I will not abandon them' — the promise to the blind exiles: 'veholakhti ivrim bederekh lo yada'u, and I will lead the blind on a road they have not known.' The 'blind' (ivrim = blind people = the exiles who cannot see the path of return, a development of the blind-servant imagery of Isaiah 42:18-19, but here YHWH leads them rather than leaving them in blindness). 'Paths they have never walked' (binetivot lo yede'um adrikhem = on paths they do not know I will lead them) — the unknown terrain of the return from Babylon = the long desert route through the Syrian desert. Four transformations: (1) darkness → light; (2) rough terrain → level ground; (3) these actions are guaranteed; (4) 'I will not abandon them' (lo azavtim = I will not forsake them — the ultimate covenant promise of Deuteronomy 31:6: 'he will never leave you nor forsake you').
17: 'Those who trust in carved idols and say to cast images, You are our gods, will be turned back and completely disgraced' — the contra-promise for idol-worshipers: 'nessugu achor yevoshu voshet, they will be turned back, they will be utterly shamed.' The contrast with Israel led forward (v16) and enemies turned back (Isaiah 41:11): those who trust idols will experience the shame-reversal that idols cannot prevent. 'Those who trust in carved idols' (habbot'im bappesel = those who trust in the carved image) — the use of batach (to trust/rely on) in its wrong direction: 30:12 indicted Judah for batach on Egypt; here the idol-worshipers batach on their manufactured deities. 'Say to cast images, You are our gods' (ha'omerim lemassekha attem elohenu = who say to the molten image: you are our gods) — the verbal declaration of the idol-worshipers as their act of religious confession, parallel to Israel's 'YHWH is our God' (Deuteronomy 6:4).
18: 'Hear, you deaf! Look, you blind, and see!' — the prophetic summons to Israel's spiritual blindness and deafness: 'hashshi'um ureshmaou haivrim habitu lir'ot, hear, you deaf! Look, you blind, and see!' The sharp command (shim'u = hear! and habitu = look/gaze intently = the same imperative used in Isaiah 40:26: 'lift up your eyes') addressed to those who are already deaf and blind. The double imperative to the disabled = the paradox of prophetic summons: calling the spiritually incapacitated to use the very faculties that are impaired. The irony: just after YHWH promised to lead the blind (v16) and to open blind eyes through the Servant (Isaiah 42:7), he turns to confront Israel's actual blindness. The blind exiles who need leading are also the blind Israel who refuses to see what YHWH is doing.
19: 'Who is blind but my servant, or deaf like my messenger whom I send? Who is blind as my dedicated one, or blind as the servant of the LORD?' — the paradox of the blind Servant: 'mi iver ki im-avdi, who is blind but my servant?' The four designations of the same figure: (1) avdi = my servant; (2) malaki asher eshlag = my messenger whom I send; (3) meshullam = my dedicated/completed one (from shalem = complete, possibly 'the one at peace with YHWH' or 'the one given in exchange'); (4) eved YHWH = the servant of YHWH. All four are 'blind.' This is the paradox that many interpreters resolve by seeing two 'servants' in Isaiah: the individual Servant of Isaiah 42:1-4 (who is not blind) and the corporate servant Israel (which is blind). The corporate servant Israel was commissioned to bring light to nations (Isaiah 42:6-7) but cannot see YHWH's acts. The blindness of Israel despite divine commission is the diagnosis that necessitates the individual Servant who is not blind.
20: 'He sees many things but does not take them in; his ears are open but he does not hear' — the diagnosis of the blind servant's condition: 'ra'ot rabbbot velo tishmor paqoach oznayim velo yishma, seeing many things and not observing; opening ears and not hearing.' The two clauses are precisely parallel: (1) 'sees many things' (ra'ot rabbbot) but 'does not observe/guard' (velo tishmor = does not take notice, does not keep/heed = from shamar = to keep, watch, observe; the same root as keeping Torah) — Israel saw YHWH's historical acts (the Exodus, the Assyrian crisis, the Babylonian exile) but did not integrate them as evidence of YHWH's character; (2) 'ears are open' (paqoach oznayim = open ears) but 'does not hear' (velo yishma = does not hear with understanding) — physical hearing without spiritual comprehension. The diagnosis is not ignorance (they saw and heard plenty) but refusal of application.
21: 'It pleased the LORD, for the sake of his righteousness, to make his law great and glorious' — the divine purpose frustrated by Israel's blindness: 'YHWH chafetz lema'an tsidqo, YHWH was pleased, for the sake of his righteousness.' YHWH's purpose: 'yagdil torah veyaadir = to make the Torah great and glorious/magnificent.' The gadal (to magnify/make great) and adar (to make glorious/magnificent) applied to the Torah = the divine intention that the Torah would be Israel's glory before the nations (Deuteronomy 4:6-8: 'this is your wisdom and understanding in the sight of the nations, who will hear about all these decrees and say, "Surely this great nation is a wise and understanding people"'). The irony: YHWH's glorious intention for the Torah is frustrated by Israel's blindness and deafness. The Torah designed to reveal YHWH's wisdom to the nations is not fulfilling its purpose because Israel is not living it.
22: 'But this is a people robbed and plundered; they are all trapped in holes and hidden in dungeons; they have been made prey and there is no one to rescue them, made spoil and no one says, Give them back' — the condition of the blind servant-people: 'vehu am-shassui vevaluz, and this is a people robbed and plundered.' The graphic inventory of captivity: (1) 'shassui = robbed' (stripped of possessions); (2) 'vavaluz = plundered' (spoiled of everything); (3) 'trapped in holes' (bahadurim kulam pehuim = in holes all of them are snared); (4) 'hidden in dungeons' (ubettei kela'im hochba'u = and in prison-houses they are hidden); (5) 'made prey' (hayyu lavaz = they have become plunder); (6) 'made spoil' (ulashalal = and as spoil). Six conditions of total captivity. 'No one to rescue' (ve'ein matsil = and there is no one to deliver) and 'no one says, Give them back' (ve'ein omer hashev = and no one says: return them). The condition is both physical (Babylonian captivity) and political (no great power advocates for Israel's release).
23: 'Who among you will pay attention to this? Who will listen and hear for what is to come?' — the prophetic summons to awareness: 'mi vakhem ya'azin zot yaqshiv veyishma le'achor, who among you will pay attention to this? Who will listen and hear for the future?' The mi (who = the rhetorical challenge to find even one person in Israel who will genuinely attend to the prophetic word). The 'pay attention' (ya'azin = to give ear/incline the ear = intensive hearing, the formal call of Deuteronomy 32:1: 'give ear, O heavens, and I will speak') and 'hear for the future' (yishma le'achor = hear for what comes after) = understanding the historical moment in light of what is coming. The question is not rhetorical in the sense of expecting no answer: it genuinely asks if anyone in the community is awake to the significance of the exile as YHWH's act.
24: 'Who handed Jacob over to plunderers, and Israel to robbers? Was it not the LORD, against whom we have sinned? For they refused to walk in his ways and were not obedient to his law' — the theological answer to Israel's condition: 'mi natan lemeshishah Ya'aqov, who gave Jacob as plunder?' The question answered immediately: 'halo YHWH zu chatanu lo, was it not YHWH, against whom we have sinned?' The self-incrimination: Israel's captivity is not Babylon's achievement but YHWH's judgment. The grounding: 'velo avah bidrakhav halokh velo shame'u betorato, for they refused to walk in his ways and did not listen to his law.' The 'refusing' (lo avah = not willing, a strong volitional rejection; cf. Exodus 7:14: 'Pharaoh's heart is hard; he refuses to let the people go') and 'not listening' (velo shame'u = they did not obey/hear the Torah). The exile is traced to two specific failures: (1) refusing to walk in YHWH's ways (covenant lifestyle); (2) not listening to the Torah (covenant law). Deuteronomy 28-30's exile-theology fulfilled.
25: 'So he poured his burning anger and the force of battle upon him; it set him ablaze all around, but he did not understand; it burned him, but he took no notice' — the divine judgment and Israel's obtuseness: 'vayishpokh alaiv chema apo ve'ezuz milchamah, and he poured out on him his burning anger and the strength of battle.' The shafakh (pour out = the pouring of wine, water, or blood; Psalms 79:6: 'pour out your wrath on the nations'; Jeremiah 10:25: 'pour out your wrath on the peoples who do not know you') of chema apo (the burning anger = the fierce, heat-like anger of YHWH). 'It set him ablaze all around' (vatelabbehu missaviv = and it set him on fire all around) — the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem as divine fire (Lamentations 2:3-4: 'he has set Zion on fire like a flaming fire'). The devastating punchline: 'but he did not understand' (velo yada = and he did not know) and 'it burned him, but he took no notice' (vattivhar-bo velo yasim al-lev = it burned him and he did not lay it on his heart). The exile itself — the most extreme divine act of discipline — did not produce understanding in Israel.
